docs.title: Bed Layout, pH Zones & Plant Compatibility
docs.text: Home | Layout | Construction | Build Guide | Materials | Soil | Drainage | Checklist | Winterberry | Azalea | Bluebells | Phlox

docs.title: The Compatibility Challenge
docs.text: Your four plants have conflicting requirements that must be designed around. The L-shape is actually ideal — it creates two natural zones.

docs.title: Compatibility Matrix
docs.text: Plant pH Sun Water Drainage Winter Red Winterberry 3.5–6.0 (acid) Full sun (6+ hrs) Moist to wet Moderate Mandarin Lights Azalea (x2) 4.5–5.5 (acid) Morning sun, PM shade Evenly moist Good Virginia Bluebells (x2) 6.5–7.5 (neutral) Part to full shade Moist spring, dry dormant Good Snowflake Carpet Phlox 6.0–7.0 (neutral) Full sun (6+ hrs) Well-drained, moderate Excellent (critical)

docs.title: Key Conflicts
docs.text:
1. pH: Winterberry + Azalea need acid (pH 4.5–5.5). Bluebells + Phlox need neutral (pH 6.0–7.5). A 1–2 unit gap.
  2. Sun: Winterberry + Phlox want full sun. Bluebells want shade. Azalea wants morning sun / PM shade.
  3. Water: Bluebells need NO water during summer dormancy. Everything else needs consistent summer moisture.
  4. Drainage: Phlox needs the fastest-draining soil. Winterberry tolerates boggy conditions.

docs.title: Recommended Layout
docs.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │           LONG SIDE (60" x 30")              │
                    │                                              │
                    │   ACID ZONE (pH 4.5–5.5)                     │
                    │                                              │
                    │   ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐   │
                    │   │                                      │   │
                    │   │   WINTERBERRY 'Winter Red'            │   │
                    │   │   (center-back, full sun side)        │   │
                    │   │                                      │   │
                    │   │   AZALEA 'Mandarin Lights' x 2       │   │
                    │   │   (flanking winterberry,              │   │
                    │   │    morning sun with some PM shade     │   │
                    │   │    from the winterberry as it grows)  │   │
                    │   │                                      │   │
                    │   └──────────────────────────────────────┘   │
                    │                                              │
                    │   Pine bark mulch throughout                  │
                    │   Soil: pine bark fines + peat + perlite     │
       ┌────────────┤                                              │
       │            └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │ SHORT SIDE
       │ (48" x 24")        ┌──────────┐
       │                    │ BURIED   │
       │  NEUTRAL ZONE      │ pH       │
       │  (pH 6.0–7.0)      │ BARRIER  │
       │                    └──────────┘
       │
       │  VIRGINIA BLUEBELLS x 2
       │  (shaded side of bed,
       │   mark locations for dormancy,
       │   interplant with hostas/ferns)
       │
       │  SNOWFLAKE CARPET PHLOX
       │  (outer edge, cascading over wall,
       │   gritty well-draining sub-zone)
       │
       │  Leaf mulch + compost (no mulch on phlox crown)
       │  Soil: topsoil + grit + perlite
       │
       └───────────────────────────────────────

docs.title: Why This Layout Works
docs.text: Decision Reason Winterberry in center-back of long leg Gets the most sun; tallest plant (6–8') anchors the back Azaleas flanking winterberry Share the same acid soil zone; get morning sun with PM shade from the winterberry as it matures Bluebells in short leg Potentially shadier side; neutral soil; separated from acid zone Phlox at outer edge of short leg Full sun position; cascades over the wall; gritty sub-zone with best drainage; near weep holes Acid zone in long leg Larger volume (25 cu ft) gives more buffering capacity for pH management Neutral zone in short leg Smaller volume (16 cu ft) is adequate for 2 small perennials + 1 ground cover pH barrier at junction Prevents acid amendments from migrating to neutral zone

docs.title: Physical Barrier
docs.text:
At the junction between the two L-legs, install a buried vertical barrier:

  • Material: 6–12" strips of rigid HDPE root barrier or cedar board
  • Position: Standing vertically in the soil at the junction point
  • Depth: Full soil column depth (16–18" after gravel layer)
  • Purpose: Minimizes lateral pH migration between zones

docs.title: Zone-Specific Soil Mixes
docs.text:
Long leg (Acid Zone — Winterberry + Azalea): - 30–40% aged blended compost - 20–30% pine bark fines (≤1/4") - 20–25% peat moss or coir - 15–20% perlite or horticultural grit - Elemental sulfur per soil test → target pH 4.5–5.5 - Surface mulch: pine bark, pine needles, or oak leaves (2–3")

Short leg (Neutral Zone — Bluebells + Phlox): - 40% quality loamy topsoil - 25% aged compost - 20% coarse grit or pea gravel - 15% coarse perlite - No acidifying amendments - Surface: leaf mulch over bluebells, NO mulch on phlox crown

Phlox sub-zone (at the edge): - 40% topsoil - 30% coarse grit (3/8") - 20% chunky perlite - 10% coarse horticultural sand - Drains within 30–60 seconds

docs.title: Annual pH Maintenance
docs.text:
Zone Test When Target If Too High If Too Low Acid zone Early spring 4.5–5.5 Apply elemental sulfur or iron sulfate Unlikely — add small amount of dolomitic lime Neutral zone Early spring 6.0–7.0 Unlikely Add small amount of lime if drifting acid

Concrete pH drift: The patio leaches lime upward. This may actually help the neutral zone stay neutral, but will fight the acid zone. Budget for annual sulfur applications in the acid zone.

docs.title: Within the Acid Zone (Long Leg)
docs.text:
Winterberry and azaleas share the same requirements — they're excellent companions. Both want: - Acid soil (pH 4.5–6.0) - Consistent moisture - Similar fertilizer (Holly-Tone)

Additional acid-zone companions if space permits: - Blue flag iris (Iris virginica) — wet, acidic, full sun - Native ferns (Osmunda regalis) — acidic wet soil - Heuchera — acid-tolerant foliage interest - Blueberries — identical pH needs

docs.title: Within the Neutral Zone (Short Leg)
docs.text:
Bluebells go dormant June–February. Companions must cover the gap:

Season What's Visible Feb–April Bluebells blooming + phlox blooming (overlapping spring show!) May Phlox being sheared; bluebells fading; hostas expanding Jun–Sep Phlox mat (green); hostas/ferns covering bluebell spot Oct–Jan Phlox mat (semi-evergreen green texture); hostas dormant; bed quiet

Best dormancy-coverage companions for the bluebell area: - Hostas — expand perfectly as bluebells decline - Hardy ferns — unfurl in late spring - Shallow-rooted annuals (impatiens, begonias) — plant carefully over dormant crowns

docs.title: Pollinator Setup
docs.text: Winter Red winterberry requires a male pollinator for berries.

docs.title: 'Southern Gentleman' Placement
docs.text:
• Place in a separate container within 50 feet of 'Winter Red'
  • 18–24" deep container, minimum 20" diameter
  • Same acid soil mix as the raised bed acid zone
  • Same watering and fertilizing schedule as 'Winter Red'
  • One 'Southern Gentleman' pollinates up to 5–20 females

Do NOT use 'Jim Dandy' — it's an early-blooming male. 'Winter Red' is late-blooming. Bloom times must overlap for pollination.

docs.title: Seasonal Calendar — All Plants Combined
docs.text: Month Acid Zone (Winterberry + Azalea) Neutral Zone (Bluebells + Phlox) Jan Dormant. Winterberry berries persisting. Dormant. Phlox semi-evergreen. Mark bluebell locations. Feb Buds swelling. Light watering if dry. Bluebell shoots emerging. Phlox fertilizer application. Mar Apply Holly-Tone to both. Azalea buds swelling. Test pH. Bluebells in active growth. Phlox beginning to bloom. Water both. Apr Azalea peak bloom (orange). Winterberry leafing out. Water regularly. Bluebell peak bloom (blue) + Phlox peak bloom (white). Spectacular spring display! May Winterberry flowering (small white). Post-bloom azalea pruning + fertilizer. Shear phlox by 1/3–1/2. Bluebells fading — stop watering their zone. Jun Green winterberry berries forming. Azalea in active growth. Monitor lace bugs. Bluebells fully dormant. Mark locations. Phlox in summer mode. Jul Deep water 2–3x/week. No fertilizer. Monitor mites. Water phlox every 3–5 days. Do NOT water bluebell area. Aug Continue deep watering. Winterberry berries swelling. Continue phlox watering. Bluebell area dry and dormant. Sep Berries turning red. Optional light azalea fertilizer (iron sulfate). Reduce water. Reduce phlox watering. Good time to divide bluebells if needed. Oct Winterberry leaves dropping — red berries on bare stems. Apply mulch to azalea. Apply leaf mulch over bluebell area. Phlox settling into winter mode. Nov Peak winterberry display. One deep watering post-frost if dry. Quiet. Phlox green mat provides winter texture. Dec Cut winterberry stems for holiday arrangements. Dormant.

docs.title: Watering Zones — Important for Irrigation Planning
docs.text:
The bed has three distinct watering regimes that need separate management:

Zone Spring Summer Fall Winter Acid zone (winterberry + azalea) 2x/week 2–3x/week (critical) 1–2x/week → reduce Monthly Bluebell area 2–3x/week NONE (dormant!) None None Phlox area Every 5–7 days Every 3–5 days Every 10–14 days Every 3–4 weeks

Drip irrigation recommendation: If installing drip lines, zone them so the bluebell area can be shut off independently in summer. Running summer irrigation over dormant bluebell roots will cause rot.

docs.title: Quick Reference Card
docs.text: Plant Qty Zone pH Sun Key Rule Winter Red Winterberry 1 Acid (long leg) 3.5–6.0 Full sun Needs male pollinator; drought = no berries Mandarin Lights Azalea 2 Acid (long leg) 4.5–5.5 AM sun / PM shade Never fertilize after July 1; never use aluminum sulfate Virginia Bluebells 2 Neutral (short leg) 6.5–7.5 Shade No water during summer dormancy; mark locations Snowflake Carpet Phlox 1 Neutral (short leg edge) 6.0–7.0 Full sun Gritty well-draining soil; shear 1/3–1/2 after bloom

docs.title: Build Guide — L-Shaped Raised Bed Construction
docs.text:
Home | Layout | Construction | Build Guide | Materials | Soil | Drainage | Checklist | Winterberry | Azalea | Bluebells | Phlox

This guide provides the step-by-step sequence for constructing the L-shaped raised bed on a concrete patio using Belgard DESIGNFORMS Scandina Grey panels.

docs.title: Shopping List
docs.text:
• Wall Panels: ~25 Large panels (12"x24"x2.6") for 2 courses (24" height).
  • Cap Panels: ~11 Small panels (6"x24"x2.6") for a concrete cap ledge.
  • Hardware: 3–4 Belgard DESIGNFORMS Hardware Kits (includes brackets, connectors, screws, nuts).
  • Drainage: ~7 cu ft of 3/4" washed gravel (limestone-free), ~21 sq ft of 1/2" mesh galvanized hardware cloth, ~21 sq ft of non-woven geotextile fabric.
  • Piping: 3" perforated PVC pipe (length for base grid).
  • pH Barrier: 6–12" strips of rigid HDPE root barrier or cedar board.
  • Soil Components: ~17 cu ft aged blended compost, ~17 cu ft pine bark fines/peat/perlite mix, ~7 cu ft loamy topsoil.
  • Amendments: Elemental sulfur (for acid zone).

See Building Materials for full product details and item numbers.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• Power drill with 1/4" socket adaptor.
  • 7/16" hex socket (minimum 1-3/4" deep).
  • 1–1.5" masonry/hammer drill bit.
  • Masonry saw or angle grinder with diamond blade (for panel cuts).
  • Penetrating concrete sealer (Siloxa-Tek or RadonSeal).

docs.title: 2. Site Prep
docs.text:
1. Clean & Seal: Pressure wash the patio footprint. Apply penetrating concrete sealer to the bed area to prevent staining and lime leaching.
  2. Layout Marking: Mark the interior footprint on the concrete.
  3. Long side: 60" x 30"
  4. Short side: 18" x 24"
  5. Air Gap: Place 1/2" spacer blocks or bed feet along the perimeter to allow lateral water flow under the frame.

docs.title: 3. Wall Assembly
docs.text: Assemble the L-shape using the DESIGNFORMS hardware system. Each course is 12" tall; use 2 courses for a 24" total height.

docs.title: Assembly Steps
docs.text:
1. Base Course: Lay 11 panels per course.
  2. Back: 3 panels (cut one to 12").
  3. Right (Long): 2 panels (cut one to 6").
  4. Step: 2 panels (cut one to 12").
  5. Right (Short): 1 panel (cut to 18").
  6. Bottom: 1 panel (24").
  7. Left: 2 panels (48").
  8. Fasten: Use joining brackets for straight runs and connectors for corners. Secure with screws and nuts using the 7/16" socket.
  9. Second Course: Repeat the layout, staggering joints where possible for stability.
  10. Cap: Lay small panels flat on top of the wall as a ledge.

See Raised Bed Construction for joining details.

docs.title: 4. Drainage Layer
docs.text:
1. Hardware Cloth: Install 1/2" mesh galvanized hardware cloth as a basket inside the frame, securing it to the inner walls.
  2. Weep Holes: Drill 1–1.5" holes through the panels 1–2" above the base, every 12–16" around the perimeter.
  3. Gravel & Pipe: Add a 3–4" layer of 3/4" washed gravel. Embed 3" perforated PVC pipe in the gravel, routing it toward the weep holes.
  4. Fabric: Layer non-woven geotextile fabric over the gravel to prevent soil migration.

See Drainage Engineering for the full analysis.

docs.title: 5. pH Barrier
docs.text:
1. Location: Install the barrier at the junction between the long leg (Acid Zone) and short leg (Neutral Zone).
  2. Installation: Stand 6–12" strips of HDPE or cedar board vertically. Ensure it spans the full soil column depth (16–18") above the drainage layer.

docs.title: 6. Soil Fill
docs.text: Fill the two zones with their respective recipes. Total soil volume is ~34 cu ft after the drainage layer.

docs.title: Acid Zone (Long Leg — ~21 cu ft)
docs.text:
• 35% aged blended compost.
  • 25% pine bark fines (≤1/4").
  • 20% peat moss.
  • 20% perlite or horticultural grit.
  • Mix in elemental sulfur to target pH 4.5–5.5.

docs.title: Neutral Zone (Short Leg — ~13 cu ft)
docs.text:
• 40% loamy topsoil.
  • 25% aged compost.
  • 20% coarse grit or pea gravel.
  • 15% coarse perlite.
  • Phlox Sub-Zone: At the outer edge, increase grit to 30% and add 10% coarse sand for rapid drainage.

See Soil Composition for mix details.

docs.title: 7. Planting
docs.text:
1. Acid Zone:
  2. Winterberry 'Winter Red': Center-back position for full sun.
  3. Azalea 'Mandarin Lights' (x2): Flanking the winterberry.
  4. Neutral Zone:
  5. Virginia Bluebells (x2): Shaded side of the bed.
  6. Snowflake Carpet Phlox: Outer edge to cascade over the wall.

See Bed Layout & Compatibility for spacing and sun requirements.

docs.title: 8. Mulch & Finish
docs.text:
1. Mulch:
  2. Acid Zone: 2–3" of pine bark or pine needles.
  3. Neutral Zone: Leaf mulch over bluebells.
  4. Warning: Do not mulch the crown of the phlox.
  5. Watering: Perform a deep initial watering. Ensure the acid zone is thoroughly saturated.
  6. Final Check: Verify weep holes are clear and water flows freely from the base.

docs.title: Building Materials — Belgard DESIGNFORMS Scandina Grey
docs.text:
Home | Layout | Construction | Build Guide | Materials | Soil | Drainage | Checklist | Winterberry | Azalea | Bluebells | Phlox

https://www.lowes.com/search?searchTerm=belgard&refinement=4104834520

docs.title: System Overview
docs.text: DESIGNFORMS is a large-format modular concrete panel system. Panels are mechanically fastened with stainless steel hardware — no mortar, no adhesive. The system uses two panel sizes and a hardware kit.

docs.title: Panel Dimensions
docs.text: Part Height (wall height per course) Length (along wall) Thickness (wall depth) Large panel 12" 24" 2.6" Small panel 6" 24" 2.6"

docs.title: Large Panels
docs.text:
Belgard 2.6-in H x 24-in L x 12-in D DESIGNFORMS Scandina Grey Concrete Interlocking

Item #5132308 | Model #10996059

  • Wall height per panel: 12"
  • 2 courses = 24" wall height (target for this bed)

docs.title: Small Panels
docs.text:
Belgard 2.6-in H x 24-in L x 6-in D DESIGNFORMS Scandina Grey Concrete Interlocking

Item #5132309 | Model #10995089

  • Wall height per panel: 6"
  • Used for: half-height courses, stepped accents, or concrete caps laid flat

docs.title: Caps
docs.text: DESIGNFORMS does not have a separate cap product. Belgard's own project guides use two capping methods:

docs.title: Option A: Concrete Cap (same panels laid flat)
docs.text:
Use the same DESIGNFORMS panels rotated 90° and laid flat on top of the wall as a ledge. A small panel (6" wide) laid flat creates a 6" wide cap ledge at 2.6" thick — gives a clean concrete edge to lean on while gardening. A large panel (12" wide) creates a wider ledge suitable for sitting or setting tools.

  • Pros: matches the wall perfectly, no additional materials, clean modern look
  • Cons: adds 2.6" to total wall height, heavy
  • Used in Belgard's "Concrete Top" project configurations

docs.title: Option B: Wood Cap
docs.text:
Cedar or composite boards fastened to the top of the panel walls via the hardware brackets. This is what Belgard uses in their "Wood Cap" project kits.

  • Pros: warmer feel, easier to lean on, lighter, can overhang walls for visual interest
  • Cons: wood degrades over time, different material from walls
  • Material: cedar 2x6 or 2x8 boards, cut to fit perimeter
  • Used in Belgard's "Wood Cap" project configurations (Lowes sells DESIGNFORMS project kits with wood caps)

docs.title: Option C: No Cap (open top)
docs.text: Leave the top course of wall panels as-is. Common for garden beds where the cap is purely aesthetic.

docs.title: Hardware Kit
docs.text:
Belgard DESIGNFORMS Hardware Kit — sold separately at Lowes

Item # — verify at store (search "DESIGNFORMS hardware kit" at Lowes or ask an associate)

docs.title: Contents per kit
docs.text: Part Qty per kit Purpose Joining Bracket 12 Connect panels edge-to-edge along a wall run Connector 18 Link panels at corners and junctions Head Screw 21 Fasten brackets and connectors Washer 5 Load distribution Nut 21 Secure screws

docs.title: Tools Required
docs.text:
• Hex socket 7/16" with 1/4" drive (minimum 1-3/4" deep)
  • Socket adaptor 1/4"
  • Power drill

docs.title: Bed Dimensions (interior planting area)
docs.text: Section Length Width Height Soil Volume Long side 60" 30" 24" 25 cu ft Short side 18" 24" 24" 6 cu ft Total 31 cu ft

docs.title: Wall Segments
docs.text: Wall Length Panels per course Notes Back (top of long side) 60" 3 Cut one panel to ~12" Right of long side 30" 2 Cut one panel to ~6" Step (bottom of long side) 36" 2 Cut one panel to ~12" Right of short side 18" 1 Cut one panel to ~18" Bottom of short side 24" 1 Exact fit Left (full L height) 48" 2 Exact fit Per course 216" 11

docs.title: Panel Counts
docs.text:
Component Calculation Count Large panels (wall courses) 11 panels/course x 2 courses (12"+12"=24") 22 Cutting waste (~15%) 22 x 0.15 +3 Large panels total ~25 Cap panels (if concrete cap) 11 panels x 1 course ~11 small panels Hardware kits ~11 panels/course, 2–3 courses, 6 corners 3–4 kits

These are estimates. Corner construction consumes or saves panels depending on how walls butt together. Buy 10–15% extra and return unused.

docs.title: Panel Weight
docs.text: Each DESIGNFORMS panel weighs approximately 25–35 lbs. Budget for total panel weight of ~1,000–1,200 lbs (on top of soil, gravel, and water weight — see raised-bed-construction.md for full weight analysis).

docs.title: Alternative Estimate: Standard CMU (Cinder Block)
docs.text: Standard concrete masonry units as a budget alternative to DESIGNFORMS. Available at Home Depot, Lowes, and masonry supply yards.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
• https://www.homedepot.com/c/ab/standard-cinder-block-dimensions/9ba683603be9fa5395fab9012213e530
  • https://www.archtoolbox.com/cmu-sizes-shapes-finishes/

docs.title: CMU Dimensions
docs.text:
Nominal dimensions include a 3/8" mortar joint. Actual block is 3/8" smaller in each direction. Everything in CMU construction works on an 8" module — 8" tall courses, 16" long blocks (2 × 8"), 8" half blocks.

Block Nominal (D x H x L) Actual (D x H x L) Use 8" Full Block 8" x 8" x 16" 7-5/8" x 7-5/8" x 15-5/8" Standard wall unit 8" Half Block 8" x 8" x 8" 7-5/8" x 7-5/8" x 7-5/8" Corners, wall ends 4" Solid Cap Block 4" x 8" x 16" 3-5/8" x 7-5/8" x 15-5/8" Top cap course

docs.title: Why 8" CMU
docs.text: Size Wall Thickness Stability at 24" Notes 4" 3-5/8" Marginal Too thin at 3 courses without reinforcement 6" 5-5/8" Adequate Lighter, but less common at box stores 8" 7-5/8" Good Standard for garden beds. Everywhere. Cheapest.

docs.title: Wall Height
docs.text:
3 courses × 8" nominal = 24" nominal wall height — exact match for this bed.

  • Actual block height: 3 × 7-5/8" + 2 × 3/8" mortar joints = 23-5/8"
  • With 4" solid cap block on top: 23-5/8" + 3-5/8" = 27-1/4" total
  • Without cap: 23-5/8" (~24")

docs.title: Assembly Method
docs.text:
Two options for a garden bed:

Method Pros Cons Dry-stack + adhesive No mortar mixing, easy DIY, can disassemble later Less rigid, adhesive costs add up Mortared (running bond) Strongest, most permanent, traditional look Requires mortar skill, can't easily undo

For dry-stack: use landscape block adhesive (e.g. Loctite PL 500) between each course. Stack blocks straight (no stagger needed). Simpler than running bond.

For mortared: stagger each course by 8" (half a block). Use Type S mortar. Alternate corner blocks between courses for interlocking.

docs.title: Footprint Impact — The Big Tradeoff
docs.text:
8" CMU walls are 7-5/8" thick vs. DESIGNFORMS at 2.6". For the same interior planting area, the CMU bed takes much more patio space:

Section Interior DESIGNFORMS exterior 8" CMU exterior Long side 60" x 30" ~65" x 35" ~76" x 46" Short side 18" x 24" ~23" x 29" ~34" x 40"

The 8" CMU long side footprint is 76" x 46" vs. 65" x 35" for DESIGNFORMS — an extra ~11" in each direction. If patio space is tight, this matters.

docs.title: Block Count
docs.text:
Same L-shape perimeter (216" interior). Each full block covers 16" of wall.

Wall Length Full blocks Half blocks Notes Back 60" 3 1 Half + 4" cut, or cut 1 full to 12" Right of long side 30" 1 1 Half + 6" cut Step 36" 2 0 + cut 1 full to 4" (wasteful) Right of short side 18" 1 0 + cut 1 full to 2" (wasteful) Bottom of short side 24" 1 1 Exact fit Left 48" 3 0 Exact fit Per course 216" ~11 ~3 Plus offcuts

Module alignment note: Only the 48" and 24" walls fall cleanly on the 8" module. The 60", 30", 36", and 18" walls all require cuts. The 36" and 18" walls produce especially awkward small offcuts (4" and 2"). Offcuts from longer walls can sometimes fill shorter ones — plan cut order to minimize waste.

docs.title: Total Block Estimate
docs.text: Component Calculation Count Full blocks (3 wall courses) ~11/course × 3 ~33 Half blocks (3 wall courses) ~3/course × 3 ~9 Cutting waste (~15%) +6 Cap blocks (4" solid, top course) ~14 ~14 Total blocks ~62

docs.title: Rough Cost Comparison
docs.text:
Material DESIGNFORMS Standard 8" CMU Wall blocks/panels ~25 panels × ~$10 = ~$250 ~48 blocks × ~$2.25 = ~$108 Cap ~11 small panels × ~$10 = ~$110 ~14 cap blocks × ~$2 = ~$28 Hardware/mortar ~4 kits × ~$35 = ~$140 Mortar (~3 bags × $9) + adhesive = ~$35 Total materials ~$500 ~$170 Assembly Bracket system (fast, no mess) Mortar or adhesive (slower, messier) Aesthetics Clean modern panels Traditional block (can be painted/stuccoed)

Prices are approximate Home Depot / Lowes 2025–2026 retail.

docs.title: CMU Shapes to Consider
docs.text:
Beyond standard stretcher blocks, useful shapes for a garden bed:

  • Double corner blocks — clean finished look on exposed corners (both faces smooth)
  • Cap blocks (4" solid) — flat solid blocks for the top course
  • Half blocks — factory-cut halves are cleaner than field-cutting full blocks

Standard gray CMU can be painted with masonry paint for a cleaner look, or parged with stucco for a smoother finish.

docs.title: CMU Notes
docs.text:
• pH leaching: CMU is standard concrete with portland cement — it will leach lime and raise pH over time, same concern as DESIGNFORMS but with thicker walls = more surface contact with soil. Budget for more aggressive sulfur amendments in the acid zone.
  • Drainage holes: Leave mortar joints open at the base course (every 2–3 blocks) instead of drilling. Or drill through blocks with a masonry bit. Hollow-core blocks also allow some vertical drainage through the cores.
  • Thermal mass: 8" CMU stores significantly more heat than 2.6" DESIGNFORMS panels. On a concrete patio in zone 7b–8a, this means the bed interior gets hotter in summer. More mulch, more water.
  • Weight: 8" CMU weighs ~33–38 lbs each. 62 blocks ≈ 2,000–2,350 lbs of block alone (vs. ~750–875 lbs for DESIGNFORMS panels). Combined with soil and gravel, total bed weight will be higher — verify patio slab capacity.

docs.title: Concrete vs. Cedar — Design Decision
docs.text:
The raised-bed-construction.md file discusses cedar and ACQ lumber. DESIGNFORMS concrete panels are an alternative with different tradeoffs:

Factor Cedar Lumber DESIGNFORMS Concrete Insulation Better (80–90°F surface) Concrete heats up more, but 2.6" wall is thin Lifespan 10–20 years 25+ years (concrete doesn't rot) Assembly Screws and corner posts Hardware kit brackets (no mortar) Aesthetics Warm, natural Clean, modern, grey Drainage holes Drill through wood Drill through concrete (masonry bit) Modification Easy to cut/modify Harder to cut (need masonry saw)

docs.title: Cutting Panels
docs.text: Panels can be cut with a masonry saw (wet saw or angle grinder with diamond blade). Wear eye and ear protection, work outdoors, wet-cut to control dust.

docs.title: pH Consideration
docs.text: Unlike poured concrete walls or blocks with limestone aggregate, DESIGNFORMS panels are high-strength concrete. They will still leach some lime over time, which raises soil pH — the same concern documented in soil-composition.md. The 2.6" wall thickness means less surface area in contact with soil compared to thick-walled block construction, but annual pH testing and sulfur amendments remain essential for the acid zone.

docs.title: Drainage Holes
docs.text: Weep holes must be drilled through the concrete panels (see drainage-engineering.md). Use a 1–1.5" masonry/hammer drill bit. Pre-drill pilot holes. Position 1–2" above the base, every 12–16" around the perimeter.
