docs.title: Drainage Engineering for Raised Beds on Concrete
docs.text: Home | Layout | Construction | Build Guide | Materials | Soil | Drainage | Checklist | Winterberry | Azalea | Bluebells | Phlox

docs.title: The Core Problem
docs.text:
On concrete, water has nowhere to go — it cannot infiltrate the ground. It must either be absorbed by the soil, evaporate, or run off across the patio surface. Two opposing risks exist simultaneously:

  1. Root rot if the bottom of the bed pools and stays saturated
  2. Rapid drying because there's no capillary moisture wicking from the ground

"Once the raised bed dries out, there's no moisture below to wick up. There's no banking system from which to draw up moisture." — Amy Stross, Tenth Acre Farm

docs.title: The Science: Perched Water Table
docs.text: This is foundational to all drainage decisions.

docs.title: What It Is
docs.text:
When you water a bed, gravity pulls water down. But near the bottom, capillary forces equal gravity, creating a permanently saturated zone called the perched water table. Its height is determined by soil particle size — not bed height or shape.

"The size and shape of the pot do not affect the height of the perched water table." — Robert Pavlis, Garden Myths (citing soil science literature)

docs.title: Key Principles
docs.text:
• Smaller particles = taller saturated zone. Heavy peat or clay mixes create tall saturated zones.
  • Coarser particles = shorter saturated zone. Perlite, pine bark fines, and grit reduce it.
  • Your 24" depth is protective. Even with a 4–6" saturated base zone, the top 18"+ of well-structured soil provides the aerated root zone your plants need.
  • Mix amendments throughout the soil column — never layer them. Iowa State Extension warns that distinct layers of different soil types impede water movement and discourage root growth.

docs.title: How to Minimize It
docs.text: "You can decrease the perched water table in any soil by increasing the average particle size. Adding sand, perlite, or vermiculite to most potting soil will accomplish this. The key is to mix it in with the soil, not to lay it at the bottom." — Garden Myths

docs.title: The Gravel Layer Debate — Settled Science (2024)
The Original "Myth"
docs.text:
WSU Extension (Linda Chalker-Scott, Ph.D.) demonstrated that gravel layers at the bottom of containers don't improve drainage and can actually retain MORE moisture in the soil above:

"Nearly 100 years ago, soil scientists demonstrated that water does not move easily from layers of finer textured materials to layers of more coarse textured."

docs.title: The 2024 Update
docs.text:
A peer-reviewed study by Avery Rowe (PLOS ONE, 2024–2025) showed drainage material does reduce water in the soil column, but with critical caveats:

  • Effects were greatest in highly porous soilless mixes (coir/perlite blends)
  • A 60mm (~2.3") sand layer was most effective at reducing water retention
  • Thicker layers outperformed thinner ones
  • The effect does NOT eliminate the perched water table — just reduces it

docs.title: The Critical Distinction for Raised Beds
docs.text:
"A 'drainage layer' of coarse material can create a zone where water can move more freely into if and only if there is a way for that water to escape beyond the bed." — Deep Green Permaculture (April 2025, citing CSU, Iowa State, NDSU, OSU Extension)

Bottom line:

Approach Verdict Gravel layer with NO drain outlets Mostly counterproductive — just moves saturated zone higher Gravel layer WITH weep holes/drain pipes Beneficial — provides reservoir and pathway for exit 60mm+ fine sand layer with drain outlets Best at reducing water retention per 2024 research Mixing amendments throughout soil Most effective overall — reduces perched water table height

docs.title: Recommended Drainage Architecture
docs.text:
CONCRETE PATIO (raise bed 1/2–1" with spacer blocks for air gap)
    |
    v
3–4" LAYER: 3/4" WASHED GRAVEL (limestone-free!)
    — 3" perforated PVC pipe embedded in gravel, routed to weep holes
    |
    v
NON-WOVEN GEOTEXTILE FABRIC (heavy-duty, not cheap woven poly)
    |
    v
SOIL MIX (16–18" effective depth)
    |
    v
2–3" ORGANIC MULCH SURFACE LAYER

docs.title: Why Each Layer Matters
docs.text:
1. Air gap (spacer blocks): Allows water exiting weep holes to flow laterally under the bed to the patio edge. Without this gap, the bottom forms a sealed pool.

  2. Gravel layer: Creates a drainage reservoir. Combined with weep holes, it provides a pathway for excess water to exit.

  3. Perforated PVC pipe: Collects water from the gravel layer and routes it to exit points. Functions as a miniature French drain within the bed.

  4. Geotextile fabric: Prevents fine soil particles from migrating into gravel and clogging it over time. Use non-woven (not woven polypropylene — that clogs faster). Place between drainage layer and soil only, not against the concrete.

  5. Soil mix: Must drain freely — high-porosity medium is essential for beds on concrete.

  6. Mulch: Slows surface water infiltration during heavy rain (meters water entry), retains soil moisture during dry periods, moderates soil temperature.

docs.title: Weep Holes — Mandatory
docs.text: Drill 1–1.5" diameter holes through the bed walls: - Position at 1–2" above the base of the bed - Space every 12–16" around the entire perimeter - Minimum 4 holes per long side - Prioritize outer corners and longest walls - Angle holes slightly downward if the wall material is thick - For the L-shaped bed, ensure holes on both the interior and exterior walls

docs.title: Why Not at the Very Bottom?
docs.text: Raising the holes slightly (1–2" from floor level) creates a thin water reservoir below them. This protects roots from extreme drought during light rain periods while still preventing sustained waterlogging.

docs.title: Option A: Perimeter Weep Holes Only (Simplest)
docs.text: Just the drilled holes described above. Adequate for most situations. Water accumulates in the gravel layer and exits laterally through the holes onto the patio.

docs.title: Option B: Slotted Drain Pipe Grid (Most Robust)
docs.text:
• Lay 3–4" perforated PVC drain pipe (Schedule 40 or corrugated agricultural tile) in a grid or diagonal pattern across the bed base
  • Pipe sits on concrete, covered by gravel layer, then soil above
  • Connect pipe to a single outlet exiting through the bed wall
  • Outlet should aim toward a patio drain or off the edge of the patio
  • This mimics a French drain adapted for a raised bed

docs.title: Landscape Fabric Between Gravel and Soil
Pros
docs.text:
• Prevents fine soil particles from migrating into gravel layer and clogging it ("piping" in drainage engineering)
  • Extends the functional life of the drainage layer
  • Allows water to pass through while filtering solids

docs.title: Cons
docs.text:
• Over time (3–5+ years), it clogs with organic matter and fine particles, reducing water flow
  • Roots can penetrate and become damaged at the boundary
  • Creates a hard boundary complicating future bed renovation

docs.title: Best Practice
docs.text:
Use non-woven geotextile fabric — not the cheap woven polypropylene sold in garden centers. Place it between the drainage layer and soil only (not against the concrete base).

Alternative: Some practitioners layer 1" of pea gravel at the very base, then the main gravel, then geotextile, then soil. Silt bypassing the fabric settles in the pea gravel rather than clogging the main drainage.

docs.title: Wicking Beds vs. Free-Draining — NOT for This Project
docs.text:
Wicking beds intentionally create a water reservoir at the base (6–12" deep) with soil wicking moisture upward. This is the opposite of what your plants need:

  • Virginia Bluebells go dormant in summer — a wicking bed's constant moisture would rot dormant corms
  • Carpet Phlox requires well-drained, even rocky/lean soil — highly susceptible to root rot in wet conditions
  • Winterberry tolerates wet but still benefits from good drainage structure
  • Azaleas — Phytophthora root rot is their #1 killer and thrives in saturated conditions

Free-draining bed with active drainage outlets is the correct architecture.

docs.title: Preventing Waterlogging on Flat Concrete
docs.text: When the concrete has no slope to a drain, you must engineer the path for water exit.

docs.title: Priority Checklist
docs.text:
1. Raise the bed slightly off concrete — 1/2–1" air gap with spacer blocks or feet allows water to flow laterally under the bed to the patio edge
  2. Drill weep holes at base of walls — see above
  3. Install perforated drain pipe in base — for maximum insurance
  4. Grade concrete if possible — even 1% slope (1/8" per foot) under the bed helps enormously. Shim the bed to create this slope if the patio is flat
  5. High-porosity soil mix — the medium itself must drain freely
  6. Overflow notch at top of wall — for catastrophic rain events, a notched board or lowered section at the top of the bed wall allows water to escape via overflow rather than saturating the entire soil column

docs.title: Pre-Placement Test
docs.text: Before building: pour 5+ gallons of water at the planned bed location and observe where it flows. If it pools with no exit path, find a better spot or create a drainage channel.

docs.title: Rain Management (Zone 7b–8a)
docs.text: Zone 7b–8a receives significant spring and fall rainfall. Engineering for this is essential.

docs.title: Strategies
docs.text:
1. Overflow escape route is mandatory. If base drain holes become overwhelmed, excess water must exit via overflow notches before reaching plant crowns.

  2. Monitor drain hole performance. After heavy rain, check whether the bed drains within 1–2 hours. If saturated more than 4 hours, the system needs additional holes or pipe capacity.

  3. Mulch surface layer — 2–3" organic mulch slows surface water infiltration, reducing the "dump" of water that overwhelms drain pipes during downpours. It meters water entry.

  4. Seasonal alignment — Virginia Bluebells go dormant by early summer, perfectly aligned with zone 7b–8a's wet spring season. They're dormant when summer drought/irrigation management is needed for other plants.

  5. Temporary rain diversion — During sustained heavy rain, a simple polycarbonate panel can divert rainfall away from the bed. Not a permanent solution but useful during known flood events.

docs.title: Raised Bed vs. Container — The Key Difference
docs.text:
"When you fill a tall, confined bed with several hundred kilograms of soil, the bottom layers will compress under the weight, squeezing soil particles closer together, reducing pore spaces. Heavily compacted soil restricts drainage by causing water to become trapped in a waterlogged layer at the base." — Deep Green Permaculture (citing multiple extension sources)

Your bed is more similar to a container than an in-ground bed: - No connection to ground soil means no downward percolation beyond the bed floor - The only exit for water is through walls (weep holes) or installed drains - Compression matters — as soil settles over years, base pore structure shrinks - Annual compost topdressing and no compaction (no walking in bed) preserves drainage

docs.title: Key Sources
docs.text: Source URL WSU Extension — Container Drainage Myth https://wpcdn.web.wsu.edu/wp-puyallup/uploads/sites/403/2015/03/container-drainage.pdf Garden Myths — Perched Water Table https://www.gardenmyths.com/perched-water-table/ Garden Myths — Gravel Science (Rowe 2024) https://www.gardenmyths.com/gravel-pots-containers-drainage/ Deep Green Permaculture — Drainage Science https://deepgreenpermaculture.com/2025/04/26/understanding-drainage-differences-in-raised-garden-beds-vs-small-containers-why-and-how-it-matters/ Homestead and Chill — Hardware Cloth Method https://homesteadandchill.com/build-raised-garden-bed-on-concrete/ Tenth Acre Farm — Pavement-Specific Guidance https://www.tenthacrefarm.com/raised-beds-pavement/ Rural Sprout — Material Comparisons https://www.ruralsprout.com/raised-beds-on-concrete/

docs.title: Plant Care and Maintenance Calendar
docs.text:
Home | Layout | Construction | Build Guide | Materials | Soil | Drainage | Checklist | Winterberry | Azalea | Bluebells | Phlox

This calendar consolidates recurring care tasks for the L-shaped raised bed in Zone 7b-8a. It covers Winter Red winterberry, Mandarin Lights azalea, Virginia bluebells, and Snowflake carpet phlox, along with general soil and drainage maintenance.

docs.title: Quick Reference: Seasonal Watering
docs.text:
Plant Spring (Mar–May) Summer (Jun–Aug) Fall (Sep–Nov) Winter (Dec–Feb) Winterberry 2x/week 2–3x/week 1–2x/week Every 2–3 weeks* Azalea Every 5–7 days Every 2–3 days Every 10–14 days Monthly* Bluebells 1–3x/week** NONE None None Phlox Every 5–7 days Every 3–5 days Every 10–14 days Every 3–4 weeks*

* Only if no significant rain; do not let roots freeze-dry in the raised bed. * Taper off in May as foliage declines; stop completely once dormant.*

docs.title: January
docs.text:
• [ ] Winterberry: Monitor berries; birds may begin feeding. Water lightly if soil is dry.
  • [ ] Azalea: Ensure 2–3" mulch is intact. No watering unless prolonged drought.
  • [ ] Bluebells: Check that location markers are visible so crowns aren't disturbed.
  • [ ] Phlox: Semi-evergreen foliage persists; no maintenance needed.
  • [ ] General: Inspect weep holes for clogs from winter debris or ice.

docs.title: February
docs.text:
• [ ] Winterberry: Prune for shaping if needed before buds swell. Remove root suckers at ground level.
  • [ ] Azalea: Apply Holly-Tone or acid-specific fertilizer as flower buds swell.
  • [ ] Bluebells: Watch for early sprouts in late February. Keep soil moist.
  • [ ] Phlox: Apply slow-release balanced fertilizer (10-10-10). Trim any brown, winter-damaged areas.
  • [ ] General: Test soil pH in both Acid and Neutral zones.

docs.title: March
docs.text:
• [ ] Winterberry: Apply Holly-Tone as buds swell. Remove any new root suckers.
  • [ ] Azalea: Peak bloom begins. Maintain consistent moisture ("damp sponge" texture).
  • [ ] Bluebells: Active growth phase. Water 2–3x/week to support vigorous shoots.
  • [ ] Phlox: Peak bloom begins. Water regularly if the top inch of soil is dry.
  • [ ] General: Apply elemental sulfur to the Acid Zone if pH has drifted above 5.5.

docs.title: April
docs.text:
• [ ] Winterberry: Monitor for leafminers as leaves emerge. Apply 2–3" acidic mulch (pine bark/needles).
  • [ ] Azalea: Peak bloom. Prune immediately after flowers fade. Deadhead spent clusters.
  • [ ] Bluebells: Peak bloom. Reduce watering slightly (1–2x/week) during flowering.
  • [ ] Phlox: Peak bloom. Ensure full sun exposure is not blocked by new growth of neighbors.
  • [ ] General: Check drainage performance after heavy spring rains; ensure no pooling at the bed base.

docs.title: May
docs.text:
• [ ] Winterberry: Flowering begins. Avoid all pesticides to protect pollinators for berry set.
  • [ ] Azalea: Main annual feeding with Holly-Tone. Monitor for lace bugs on leaf undersides.
  • [ ] Bluebells: Foliage will yellow and collapse. Taper watering to zero by month's end.
  • [ ] Phlox: Shear the entire mat back by 1/3 to 1/2 immediately after the final flowers fade.
  • [ ] General: Monitor for early pest activity (leafminers, lace bugs, aphids).

docs.title: June
docs.text:
• [ ] Winterberry: Green berries forming. Keep soil consistently moist. Top-dress mulch if needed.
  • [ ] Azalea: Active growth. Final deadline for fertilizer is July 1. Monitor for lace bugs.
  • [ ] Bluebells: Fully dormant. Do not water this zone. Do not dig or disturb the soil.
  • [ ] Phlox: Water every 3–5 days. Check for spider mites if weather is hot and dry.
  • [ ] General: Check weep holes; ensure water exits freely during summer storms.

docs.title: July
docs.text:
• [ ] Winterberry: Berries swelling. Water deeply 2–3x/week. No fertilizer during peak heat.
  • [ ] Azalea: Heat stress phase. Water every 2–3 days. Morning-only irrigation to prevent disease.
  • [ ] Bluebells: Dormant. Ensure nearby watering does not saturate the bluebell root zone.
  • [ ] Phlox: Water every 3–5 days. Monitor for spider mites (bronze leaves, fine webbing).
  • [ ] General: Maintain 2–3" mulch depth to insulate roots from concrete-radiated heat.

docs.title: August
docs.text:
• [ ] Winterberry: Critical moisture month. Drought stress now causes premature fruit drop.
  • [ ] Azalea: Monitor for lace bugs and spider mites. No fertilizer.
  • [ ] Bluebells: Dormant. No maintenance.
  • [ ] Phlox: Water regularly. Check for center die-out; plan for September division if needed.
  • [ ] General: Inspect plants for signs of heat stress or drought (scorch, wilting, fruit drop).

docs.title: September
docs.text:
• [ ] Winterberry: Berries begin turning red. Optional light feeding with Holly-Tone (early Sept only).
  • [ ] Azalea: Reduce watering frequency to help the plant harden off for winter. No fertilizer.
  • [ ] Bluebells: Ideal time to divide established clumps or add new dormant corms.
  • [ ] Phlox: Divide every 2–3 years to maintain a dense mat. Reduce watering as temps cool.
  • [ ] General: Top off soil levels if settling has occurred (expect ~15% volume loss in year 1).

docs.title: October
docs.text:
• [ ] Winterberry: Leaves drop, exposing red berries. Peak ornamental display.
  • [ ] Azalea: Apply fresh 2–3" acidic mulch. Pull mulch 5–6" away from the main stems.
  • [ ] Bluebells: Apply 2–3" leaf mulch over the planting area to mimic forest floor conditions.
  • [ ] Phlox: Semi-evergreen foliage remains green. No maintenance needed.
  • [ ] General: Clear fallen leaves from weep holes and the patio surface around the bed.

docs.title: November
docs.text:
• [ ] Winterberry: Berries persist. Cut stems for holiday arrangements (this serves as light pruning).
  • [ ] Azalea: Give one final deep watering after the first hard frost if the fall was dry.
  • [ ] Bluebells: Fully dormant.
  • [ ] Phlox: No maintenance.
  • [ ] General: Ensure all drainage outlets are clear before winter freezes.

docs.title: December
docs.text:
• [ ] Winterberry: Berries persist. Water every 2–3 weeks if there is no rain or snow.
  • [ ] Azalea: Fully dormant. Monitor mulch depth.
  • [ ] Bluebells: Fully dormant.
  • [ ] Phlox: Semi-evergreen foliage provides winter texture.
  • [ ] General: Monitor for "freeze-drying" in the raised bed; water lightly during warm winter spells.

docs.title: Signs of Trouble
docs.text: Symptom Likely Cause Action Yellow leaves, green veins High soil pH (Chlorosis) Test pH; apply elemental sulfur or chelated iron. Wilting in morning (soil moist) Root Rot (Phytophthora) Improve drainage; reduce watering; remove dead wood. Berries dropping early Summer drought stress Increase watering to 2–3x/week; check mulch depth. No berries on mature plant Lack of pollination Ensure 'Southern Gentleman' male is within 50 feet. Bronze leaves, fine webbing Spider Mites Spray leaf undersides with strong water jet or soap. Yellow stippling on leaves Lace Bugs Apply insecticidal soap or neem oil to leaf undersides. White powdery coating Powdery Mildew Improve air circulation; apply copper soap fungicide. Brown, wedge-shaped leaf patches Foliar Nematodes Remove and destroy affected plant; do not compost. Leaf notches on edges Weevils Apply entomopathogenic nematodes to soil. Bare/woody center in mat Aging or skipped pruning Divide plant in fall; ensure annual post-bloom shearing.

docs.title: Mandarin Lights Hardy Azalea — Complete Care Guide
Rhododendron 'Mandarin Lights' — Northern Lights Series
docs.text:
Home | Layout | Construction | Build Guide | Materials | Soil | Drainage | Checklist | Winterberry | Azalea | Bluebells | Phlox

Zone: 7b–8a | Hardiness: USDA 3–7 (conservative) / 4–8 (workable) | Type: Deciduous shrub Mature size: 4–5' tall x 3–4' wide | Quantity in bed: 2 plants Bred by: University of Minnesota Landscape Arboretum

docs.title: At a Glance
docs.text: Attribute Value Sun Morning sun (6 AM–1 PM), afternoon shade important in zone 7b–8a Water Consistently moist, never soggy — "damp sponge" texture Soil pH 4.5–5.5 (strictly acidic) Fertilizer Acid-specific (Holly-Tone); stop by July 1 Bloom March–April (mandarin orange, before leaves emerge) Deciduous Yes — drops all leaves in winter Cold hardiness Flower buds survive -40°F Primary zone 7b–8a challenge Summer heat, not winter cold

docs.title: Sun Needs
docs.text:
Morning sun with afternoon shade is the critical siting decision in zone 7b–8a.

Monrovia: "Shelter from harsh sun exposures in hot summer areas."

MU Extension G6825: "Filtered sunlight is ideal, but morning sunlight with shade after 1 PM is satisfactory."

ARS: "In hot areas, northern exposures are preferable to southern exposures."

docs.title: Zone 7b–8a Reality
docs.text:
Mandarin Lights was bred for extreme cold tolerance (Minnesota), not Southeast summer heat. Hot afternoon sun is the primary stressor — causes lace bug infestations, leaf scorch, and flower bud bleaching.

Recommended: - Full morning sun (6 AM–12/1 PM) — essential for flowering - Light shade or dappled shade in afternoon (1–5 PM) — especially when temps exceed 90°F - Position on the north or east side of taller plants, or where the bed structure provides afternoon relief

Important nuance: As a deciduous azalea, Mandarin Lights is more sun-tolerant than large-leaved evergreen rhododendrons. Zone 7b can likely handle full sun. Zone 8a — afternoon shade is insurance.

docs.title: Water Needs
docs.text:
Highly sensitive to both over- AND under-watering.

ARS: "Rhododendrons and azaleas ideally grow in damp, never soggy soil. Think of taking a wet sponge and squeezing it. The remaining damp sponge is the nearly perfect air and water environment for rhododendron roots."

docs.title: The Morning Droop Test
docs.text:
• Leaves droop in afternoon but recover overnight → Normal
  • Leaves still droop at dawn → Water immediately
  • Leaves wilt in morning AND soil IS moist → Root rot — usually fatal

docs.title: Seasonal Watering Schedule
docs.text: Season Frequency Notes Spring bloom (Mar–Apr) Every 5–7 days Support flowering; damp not wet Post-bloom growth (May–Jun) Every 5–7 days Consistent moisture for new foliage July–August (heat) Every 2–3 days in raised bed Raised beds on concrete dry rapidly; finger test 2" depth daily September Every 10–14 days Reduce deliberately — allow hardening for winter Oct–Nov Every 2–3 weeks One deep watering after first frost if fall was dry Winter Monthly if no rain Roots need some moisture even when dormant

docs.title: Establishment (Year 1)
docs.text:
Monrovia: "Water deeply, regularly in first growing season to establish root system."

ARS: "After transplanting the roots can take longer to grow out into the surrounding soil... if the root ball is allowed to dry out it can be difficult to get it wet again."

In a raised bed on concrete, water more frequently in year 1 since roots cannot reach groundwater. Dripping hose at the base for 2–3 hours during dry spells.

docs.title: Soil Needs — pH Is the Central Issue
pH: Strict Acid Requirement
docs.text:
MU Extension G6825: "Rhododendrons and azaleas must have an acid soil. Most of them thrive best at a soil pH between 5.0 and 5.5."

ARS: "Rhododendrons and azaleas prefer acidic soils having a pH between 4.5 to 6.0."

Target: pH 4.5–5.5. At pH 6.0+, iron and manganese become chemically unavailable → chlorosis. At pH 7.0+, plant will decline.

docs.title: Concrete pH Drift — Direct Threat
docs.text:
ARS: "Avoid planting azaleas near concrete sidewalks, driveways or foundations that may leach out lime which raises the pH."

Your concrete patio WILL leach lime into the bed over time. Test pH annually and amend proactively.

docs.title: Soil Composition
docs.text:
ARS: "About half of the planting medium should be organic material. Combinations of sphagnum peat moss, pine or fir bark fines, compost, and aged, chopped leaves should be worked into the soil."

Pine bark is critical:

ARS: "Pine bark is particularly good because substances in the pine bark are thought to inhibit fungi that cause root rot."

MU Extension: "Avoid using a large amount of peat, especially for rhododendrons, because peat-amended soil may hold excessive moisture during wet conditions."

docs.title: Drainage — Also Strict
docs.text:
ARS: "Planting in raised beds is the best solution in heavy soils."

Your raised bed setup is actually the ARS-recommended approach for azalea cultivation. The key is ensuring the bed drains well.

docs.title: What NOT to Use
docs.text:
• Aluminum sulfate — TOXIC to azalea roots (confirmed ARS and MU Extension)
  • General garden fertilizers — use acid-specific only
  • Lime or limestone in any form
  • Tap water if pH > 7.5 (use rainwater instead)

docs.title: Nutrient Needs — Azalea-Specific Protocol
docs.text: ARS: "Unlike other shrubs, azaleas are shallow rooted and can be easily injured by excess fertilizer. Some experienced growers do not apply chemical fertilizers at all."

docs.title: What to Use
docs.text:
MU Extension: "Avoid using general garden fertilizers. Use those specially formulated for acid-loving plants. Fertilizers supplying nitrogen in the ammonium form are best."

Best products: - Holly-Tone (Espoma) — organic granular, industry standard - Azalea/Camellia/Rhododendron fertilizers - Cottonseed meal — organic nitrogen, recommended by ARS and MU Extension - Fish emulsion — good organic liquid option

MU Extension: "A fertilizer analysis similar to 6-10-4 applied at 2 pounds per 100 square feet is usually adequate."

docs.title: Schedule
docs.text: When Action March–April Light application as buds swell (supports bloom) Post-bloom (May) Main annual feeding with acid fertilizer STOP by July 1 Late feeding promotes tender growth vulnerable to heat AND winter damage Late fall (optional) 1.25 cups iron sulfate per 100 sq ft for winter hardening and chlorosis prevention

docs.title: Iron and Sulfur Supplementation
docs.text:
ARS: "Lack of iron causes yellowing between green-colored veins on younger leaves. Iron deficiency is frequently caused by too high a soil pH."

  • Annual elemental sulfur or ferrous (iron) sulfate to maintain pH below 5.5
  • Do NOT use aluminum sulfate — toxic to azalea roots

docs.title: Phosphorus Myth
docs.text:
ARS: "The popular perception that rhododendron and azalea flowering is enhanced by phosphorus is incorrect."

Don't over-apply phosphorus. Stick to acid-specific formulas.

docs.title: Shallow Fibrous Roots
docs.text:
ARS: "Rhododendrons and azaleas have no tap roots. They have fibrous roots — a dense mass of hair-like roots."

ARS: "Because of their shallow roots, little or no cultivation should be done around rhododendrons. Weeds should be carefully pulled."

docs.title: Mulching — Critical Practice
docs.text:
MU Extension: "Most azaleas and rhododendrons are shallow rooted and need a heavy layer of mulch to conserve moisture and minimize winter injury."

Best mulch materials: - Pine bark chips or fines (preferred by ARS — inhibits Phytophthora) - Pine needles (classic azalea mulch — maintains acidity) - Partly decomposed oak leaves (naturally acidifying)

Depth: 2–3" year-round.

Critical rule:

ARS: "Do not pile the mulch right up to the trunk, leave 5–6 inches free of mulch."

Mulch against the crown = crown rot = plant death.

MU Extension: "Mulch should be moved away from direct contact with stems in early fall to allow hardening."

docs.title: Care & Watering Cycle (Month by Month)
docs.text: Month Activity Feb–Mar Leafless but flower buds developing. Light acid fertilizer as buds swell. Do NOT prune. Ensure moisture if winter was dry. Mar–Apr Peak bloom — mandarin orange flowers erupt before leaves. Water regularly. No fertilizer during bloom. Apr–May Primary pruning window — immediately after bloom. Main annual fertilizer application. Deadhead spent clusters. New foliage emerges. May–Jun Active growth. Maintain consistent moisture. Light shaping if desired (finish before July). Monitor for lace bugs. Final fertilizer deadline: July 1. Jul–Aug Heat stress phase. No fertilizing. Water deeply 2–3x/week. Morning-only irrigation (wet foliage overnight → disease). Monitor for spider mites and lace bugs. Sep–Oct Reduce watering — allow hardening. Apply fresh 2–3" mulch (pine bark/needles). No fertilizer. Pull mulch away from stems. November One deep watering after first frost if fall was dry. Replenish mulch. Dec–Jan Fully dormant and leafless. Flower buds visible as small nubs. No watering unless drought. Monitor mulch depth.

docs.title: Pruning
docs.text:
ARS: "Rhododendrons and azaleas are pruned after they bloom. Pruning after buds are set removes next year's flowers."

  • Window: Immediately after bloom (April–May) through early summer — never after July 1
  • Deciduous azaleas can be cut anywhere and will branch from that point
  • Pinch soft new shoots for denser form (before July)
  • Deadhead spent flower clusters for appearance and disease prevention
  • Rejuvenation: If leggy after many years, remove 1/3 of old wood per year over 3 years

docs.title: Cold: Non-Issue
docs.text:
MU Extension: "Plants and flower buds are said to have survived -40 degrees Fahrenheit."

Zone 7b minimum temps (5–10°F) are nothing to this plant. Winter cold is NOT the challenge.

docs.title: Heat: The Actual Challenge
docs.text:
Mandarin Lights was bred for cold, not heat. Zone 8a summer heat (90–100°F) is the primary stressor:

  1. Lace bug pressure increases in sunny, hot exposures
  2. Phytophthora root rot is more aggressive in hot, wet conditions
  3. Flower bud bleaching from intense summer sun
  4. Summer leaf scorch from heat + wind + drying

docs.title: Heat Mitigation
docs.text:
• Afternoon shade (the single most impactful strategy)
  • Deep organic mulch (3") to keep roots cool
  • Morning-only irrigation — never wet foliage at night in summer
  • Pine bark in soil mix (suppresses Phytophthora)

ARS: "In hot climates, raised planting beds that incorporate 50% or more fine pine bark can be helpful in suppressing Phytophthora root rot."

Since Mandarin Lights is deciduous, winter sun/wind desiccation of foliage is NOT an issue (no leaves to protect). This is an advantage over evergreen rhododendrons.

docs.title: Chlorosis (Yellowing Leaves)
docs.text:
Pattern Cause Fix Young leaves yellow, veins green Iron deficiency (pH too high) Test pH; apply elemental sulfur; chelated iron foliar spray for immediate relief Old leaves yellow, young green Magnesium deficiency Epsom salts (1 tbsp/gallon) All leaves uniformly pale Overall nutrient deficiency or pH too low Soil test

MU Extension: "If the leaves turn yellow in sections between the veins, but the veins remain green, iron deficiency is the cause."

Purdue Extension: "Iron chlorosis can usually be temporarily controlled by spraying foliage with iron sulfate solution at one ounce per gallon of water."

docs.title: Other Problems
docs.text: Problem Likely Cause Solution Wilting in morning, soil IS moist Phytophthora root rot — usually fatal Improve drainage immediately; phosphonate fungicides if caught early Bud blast (buds die without opening) Late spring freeze; drought during bud formation (Aug–Oct) Protected placement; adequate moisture during bud-setting Leaf scorch (brown edges/tips) Too much afternoon sun; drought; wind Afternoon shade; deep mulch; consistent moisture Lace bugs Sunny, hot exposure (#1 pest in zone 7b–8a) Insecticidal soap; neem oil; plant in morning-sun/afternoon-shade Branch dieback Phomopsis/Botryosphaeria fungi (drought stress) Prune well below diseased area with sterilized tools Powdery mildew Cool moist weather, poor air circulation Potassium bicarbonate; improve air circulation Failure to bloom Pruned after July 1; late frost; too much shade; excess nitrogen Time pruning correctly; 4+ hrs sun; reduce nitrogen Weevils (leaf edge notches) Adult weevils feeding at night Entomopathogenic nematodes in soil for larvae

docs.title: Lace Bugs — Primary Pest
docs.text:
ARS: "Both adults and nymphs feed on the backside of the leaf resulting in yellow spots. Lace bugs thrive in sunny locations since their natural enemies avoid these locations."

  • Identification: Flip leaf — lace-winged adults or dark excrement on underside; yellow stippling on top
  • Organic treatment: Insecticidal soap (nymphs); neem oil; forceful water spray to undersides; plant in morning-sun/afternoon-shade

docs.title: Phytophthora Root Rot — Fatal Disease
docs.text:
ARS: "Root rot is usually caused by Phytophthora. If leaves are wilted in the morning and the ground is moist, this is a symptom. It is usually fatal."

  • Prevention is everything: Excellent drainage; pine bark in soil; never overwater
  • Early stage only: Phosphonate fungicides (Aliette, Agri-Fos)

docs.title: Branch Die-back
docs.text:
ARS: "Die-back of entire branches is usually caused by Phomopsis or Botryosphaeria. These fungi thrive during dry periods when plants are stressed."

  • Prune dead/dying branches well below disease. Dispose in trash, not compost. Disinfect pruners between cuts.

docs.title: Powdery Mildew
docs.text:
ARS: "The white powder form is prevalent on deciduous azaleas."

  • Potassium bicarbonate (Bonide Remedy) — ARS-recommended
  • Sulfur-based fungicides (not above 85°F)
  • Improve air circulation; avoid overhead watering

docs.title: Weevils
docs.text:
ARS: "If leaves have notches in the outside edges, these are caused by weevils."

  • Biological control: Entomopathogenic nematodes in soil for larvae

docs.title: Spider Mites
docs.text:
• Hot, dry summer conditions
  • Insecticidal soap or neem oil; strong water jets to leaf undersides

docs.title: pH Drift Prevention Protocol (Annual)
docs.text:
This requires active management when growing azaleas on concrete.

ARS: "Rhododendrons and azaleas will let you know if the pH is not correct."

docs.title: Annual Checklist
docs.text:
1. Test pH (early spring) with a reliable meter
  2. If pH 5.5–6.0: Apply wettable sulfur OR 1.25 cups iron sulfate per 100 sq ft (MU Extension)
  3. If pH above 6.0: More aggressive correction with elemental sulfur (see table)
  4. Maintain acidic mulch — pine needles and oak leaves naturally acidify
  5. Monitor water chemistry — if tap water pH > 7.5, collect rainwater for irrigation

docs.title: Sulfur Application Table (MU Extension G6825)
docs.text: Current pH Sulfur (lbs/100 sq ft) Iron Sulfate (lbs/100 sq ft) 7.5 5.0 11.5 7.0 3.5 9.0 6.5 1.5 3.5

docs.title: Raised Bed Specific Considerations
docs.text:
1. Raised beds are the ARS-recommended approach for azaleas in challenging conditions — you're doing it right
  2. More frequent watering than in-ground — confirmed by ARS
  3. pH drift monitoring is mandatory — concrete leaches lime
  4. Soil doesn't self-replenish — top-dress with 1–2" composted pine bark every fall
  5. Root ball management — loosen roots at planting, score vertical cuts, pull outer roots outward (ARS emphasizes this)
  6. Never plant too deep — top of root ball should be several inches above surrounding soil (ARS)
  7. Temperature extremes — raised bed on concrete gets very hot in summer; deep mulching (3") essential
  8. Mature size (4–5' tall) — becomes the visual centerpiece. Position toward back/center of bed.
  9. 24" depth is adequate — shallow fibrous roots (12–24") fit well

docs.title: Good Companions (similar pH/drainage)
docs.text:
• Heuchera (coral bells) — acid-tolerant
  • Epimedium — great in partial-shade azalea zone
  • Leucothoe fontanesiana — same acid soil, evergreen, native
  • Blueberries — identical pH needs
  • Native ferns — love acid organic soil and dappled shade
  • Winterberry — same acid zone in your bed

docs.title: Conflict in Your Bed
docs.text:
• Phlox subulata (pH 6.0–7.0) — keep in separate neutral zone
  • Virginia Bluebells (pH 6.5–7.5) — keep in separate neutral zone

docs.title: Sources
docs.text: Source URL American Rhododendron Society — Soil, Planting, Irrigation, Fertilizing, Pruning, Disease https://www.rhododendron.org/ MU Extension G6825 (reviewed March 2026) https://extension.missouri.edu/publications/g6825 Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder https://www.missouribotanicalgarden.org/ Monrovia — Mandarin Lights https://www.monrovia.com/mandarin-lights-azalea.html Purdue University — Iron Chlorosis https://www.purdue.edu/hla/sites/yardandgarden/interveinal-chlorosis-on-azaleas-and-rhododendron/ Chicago Botanic Garden https://www.chicagobotanic.org/

docs.title: Raised Garden Bed on Concrete — Construction Guide
docs.text: Home | Layout | Construction | Build Guide | Materials | Soil | Drainage | Checklist | Winterberry | Azalea | Bluebells | Phlox

docs.title: Bed Specifications
docs.text:
• Shape: L-shaped (two adjoining rectangles)
  • Long side: 60" x 30" x 24" deep
  • Short side: 48" x 24" x 24" deep
  • Total soil volume: ~41 cu ft (~1.5 cu yards)
  • Zone: 7b–8a

docs.title: Materials — Use Cedar or ACQ Lumber, Not Metal
docs.text: Metal raised beds on concrete in zone 7b–8a create a double heat amplification effect — metal surfaces reach 100–120°F while concrete radiates heat from below. Cedar or modern ACQ pressure-treated lumber insulates significantly better (80–90°F surface temps).

docs.title: Material Comparison
docs.text:
Material Pros Cons Lifespan Cedar / Redwood Naturally rot-resistant, best insulation, no chemical treatment needed Highest cost 10–20 years ACQ Pressure-Treated Budget-friendly, FDA-approved for food production (no arsenic since 2004) Requires stainless steel fasteners (reacts with galvanized) 15–20 years Galvanized Steel Very durable, modern look Surface temps 100–120°F in summer; heat amplified by concrete below 15–20+ years Composite / Synthetic Excellent rot resistance, good aesthetics More expensive than cedar in most markets 20+ years

UF/IFAS Extension: "Cedar, redwood, and synthetic wood are naturally rot-resistant. Do not use railroad ties or old pressure-treated lumber purchased prior to January 2004."

Recommendation for this project: Cedar or ACQ pressure-treated wood. Avoid metal in zone 7b–8a on concrete.

docs.title: Bottom Construction — Open with Hardware Cloth Basket
docs.text: Do NOT put soil directly on concrete (washes out, concrete alkalinity affects pH). Do NOT use a sealed solid bottom (creates a bathtub that drowns roots).

docs.title: Recommended Approach (Homestead and Chill, tested method)
docs.text:
1. Install galvanized hardware cloth (1/2" mesh) as a basket inside the frame, secured to inner walls with screws or U-nails
  2. Layer commercial-duty landscape fabric on top of the hardware cloth — loose, not taut
  3. Add cross-support boards (2x4 cedar) spanning across the bottom inside the frame at midpoints

"I don't recommend placing the soil itself right on the concrete. Yet I don't necessarily recommend adding a solid bottom on the bed either. Instead, we create a wire and fabric 'basket' or bottom on our raised bed to both contain the soil but also promote drainage." — Deanna Talerico, Homestead and Chill

For the 30" wide section: Add at least one cross-board to prevent hardware cloth sagging under soil weight. For the 24" wide section: Hardware cloth alone may be sufficient, but a cross-board adds insurance.

docs.title: What NOT to Do
docs.text:
• Do not put soil directly on concrete — it washes out, makes a mess, and concrete alkalinity will affect pH
  • Do not use a completely sealed solid bottom without adequate drainage holes — creates a bathtub
  • Do not use cheap landscape fabric — the thin black plastic-like variety tears quickly; use commercial-grade woven fabric

docs.title: Option A: Two Adjacent Rectangles (Recommended)
docs.text:
Build two separate rectangular beds and join them at the corner: - Bed 1: 60" x 30" x 24" - Bed 2: 48" x 24" x 24"

Connect them at the junction with a shared 4x4" cedar corner post (cut to 24" height). Attach both sections to this post with 3" exterior screws. Pre-drill to prevent splitting.

Advantages: - Simpler construction (no complex corner joinery) - Individual sections can be repaired or replaced independently - Easier soil management if running separate pH zones (which you need) - Can be disassembled for moving

docs.title: Option B: Single Continuous L-Frame
docs.text:
Build as one structure using a 4x4" corner post at the interior L-junction. Run boards from each leg to this post. Works well but is more complex.

UF/IFAS Extension: "Beds can be various shapes (e.g., square, rectangle, L shaped, triangle)" — L-shaped beds are standard and normal.

docs.title: Critical: Interior Cross-Bracing
docs.text: At 60" length with 30" width and 24" depth, the long side needs an interior cross-brace (2x6" board spanning the width) at the 30" midpoint to resist outward bow from wet soil hydrostatic pressure. This is critical — 24" of saturated soil exerts significant lateral force.

docs.title: Volume Breakdown
docs.text: Section Dimensions Volume Long section 60" x 30" x 24" = 5' x 2.5' x 2' 25 cu ft Short section 48" x 24" x 24" = 4' x 2' x 2' 16 cu ft Total 41 cu ft

docs.title: Weight Estimates (with 4-6" gravel drainage layer)
docs.text: Component Estimated Weight Gravel layer (~10 cu ft x 100 lbs/cu ft) ~1,000 lbs Soil mix (~31 cu ft, moist at ~50 lbs/cu ft) ~1,550 lbs Cedar frame ~150–200 lbs Plants, water in pore spaces ~100 lbs Total ~2,800–2,900 lbs (~1.3–1.4 tons)

docs.title: Load Per Square Foot
docs.text:
• Footprint: ~20.5 sq ft
  • Load: ~136–140 lbs/sq ft

docs.title: Safety Assessment
docs.text:
Surface Rating Safe? Wooden residential deck 40–60 PSF NO — too heavy for a wood deck Concrete patio (standard 4" slab) 3,000–4,000+ PSI compressive YES — well within capacity

Practical guidance: - A solid concrete patio on stable ground is fine for this weight - If the patio is cracked, heaved, or shows subsidence, consult a structural engineer before loading ~1.4 tons - Position the bed at the edge closer to the house (where the slab is supported by the foundation) rather than floating in the middle of a large span

docs.title: Staining Mechanisms
docs.text:
1. Tannin runoff from cedar wood — turns concrete orange/brown
  2. Sediment/mineral runoff from soil and compost
  3. Moss and algae that grow in permanently moist shadow beneath the bed

docs.title: Mitigation Strategies
docs.text:
• Pre-seal the concrete under the bed footprint with a penetrating concrete sealer (Siloxa-Tek or RadonSeal) before placing the bed
  • Seal cedar bottom edges with food-safe wood sealer (Seal-It-Green's Garden Box Armor) to reduce tannin bleed
  • Leave a 1/2" gap under frame edges for airflow — use shims or bed feet to prevent trapped moisture and moss
  • A 4–6" gravel base layer inside the bed significantly reduces soil runoff staining

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
• Pressure washing removes most staining if you ever move the bed
  • Muriatic acid solution handles stubborn mineral stains

"If and when you ever decide to move the garden bed, it may have some discoloration below. Yet a pressure washer should be able to remove it!" — Homestead and Chill

docs.title: Thermal Considerations (Zone 7b–8a)
docs.text: Concrete is a thermal mass material — absorbs heat slowly during the day, releases it slowly at night. This creates both benefits and risks.

docs.title: Temperature Data
docs.text: Surface Peak Summer Temp Behavior Wood raised bed 80–90°F exterior Insulates soil well Metal raised bed 100–120°F exterior Heats soil rapidly Dark concrete surface 125–140°F in direct sun Radiates heat upward into bed base

docs.title: Winter/Spring Benefit
docs.text: Concrete absorbs solar heat and radiates it upward through the gravel base, keeping roots warmer at night. This can extend your growing season by 2–4 weeks in spring and fall.

docs.title: Summer Risk
docs.text: Root zone temperatures above 85–90°F cause stress in most plants. Sustained temps above 95°F can kill fine feeder roots.

docs.title: Summer Heat Mitigation
docs.text:
1. Mulch heavily — 3–4" of organic mulch on the soil surface (single most effective tool)
  2. Drip irrigation — consistent moisture provides evaporative cooling
  3. Light-colored bed material — light cedar reflects more heat than dark metal or stained wood
  4. Evening hose-down of surrounding concrete — evaporative cooling of the patio reduces ambient heat
  5. Strategic plant placement — heat-sensitive plants (bluebells) on the shaded side

Rural Sprout: "Consider your concrete color. Raised beds built on dark pavement will absorb more sunlight and heat up quickly. They will have a higher evaporation rate and need more frequent watering."

docs.title: Key Sources
docs.text: Source URL Homestead and Chill https://homesteadandchill.com/build-raised-garden-bed-on-concrete/ Tenth Acre Farm https://www.tenthacrefarm.com/raised-beds-pavement/ Rural Sprout https://www.ruralsprout.com/raised-beds-on-concrete/ UF/IFAS Extension https://edis.ifas.ufl.edu/publication/EP472 GreatGardenPlans (heat data) https://greatgardenplans.com/do-metal-raised-garden-beds-get-too-hot/

docs.title: Snowflake Carpet Phlox — Complete Care Guide
Phlox subulata 'Snowflake'
docs.text:
Home | Layout | Construction | Build Guide | Materials | Soil | Drainage | Checklist | Winterberry | Azalea | Bluebells | Phlox

Zone: 7b–8a | Hardiness: USDA 3–8b | Type: Semi-evergreen mat-forming perennial Mature size: 4–6" tall x 18–24" wide | Quantity in bed: 1 plant

docs.title: At a Glance
docs.text: Attribute Value Sun Full sun (6+ hrs) — light afternoon filter OK in zone 8a Water Moderate; drought-tolerant once established Soil pH 6.0–7.0 (flexible, neutral) Drainage CRITICAL — native to rocky slopes. Will die in wet soil. Fertilizer One slow-release application in late winter/early spring Bloom March–May (pure white flowers) Growth habit Dense, creeping mat — cascades over raised bed edges Semi-evergreen Yes — retains green foliage through winter in zone 7b–8a

docs.title: Sun Needs
docs.text:
Full sun (6+ hours) strongly preferred.

Missouri Botanical Garden: "Best flowering is in full sun, but plants generally appreciate some dappled sun in areas with hot, humid summers."

  • Minimum 4 hours for acceptable flowering (The Spruce)
  • Below 6 hours: noticeably reduced bloom, leggy growth, poor air circulation (powdery mildew risk)
  • In zone 8a, light afternoon dappling (not shade) can reduce heat stress without compromising bloom
  • Target: Full morning sun through noon at minimum

docs.title: Water Needs
docs.text:
Season Frequency Notes Early spring (Mar–Apr) Every 5–7 days Active bloom; soil moist but never wet Late spring (May) Every 7 days Post-bloom recovery Summer (Jun–Aug) Every 3–5 days in raised bed Concrete amplifies heat; check top inch — if dry, water Fall (Sep–Oct) Every 10–14 days Cooling temps reduce demand Winter (Nov–Feb) Every 3–4 weeks if no rain Minimal need

The Spruce: "Creeping phlox will likely require watering weekly during the heat of the summer."

Gardening Know How: "Water regularly but allow the soil to dry slightly between each watering. In a container, creeping phlox may rot in soggy soil."

Drought tolerance: Moderate once established. Native to "somewhat dry, rocky or sandy places" (Missouri Botanical Garden). Will NOT survive sustained waterlogging.

docs.title: Soil Needs — WELL-DRAINING ZONE
docs.text: This is the plant that needs the dedicated well-draining soil section in your bed.

docs.title: Why Drainage is Non-Negotiable
docs.text:
Missouri Botanical Garden: "Good soil drainage is important. Plants grow well in sandy or gravelly soils and tolerate hot, dry exposures better than most other species of phlox."

Missouri Botanical Garden: "Native to somewhat dry, rocky or sandy places, open woodland areas and slopes."

The species evolved on rocky Appalachian slopes with rapid water movement. Standing water = death by root rot. This is the #1 killer of phlox in raised beds with inadequate drainage.

docs.title: Well-Draining Soil Mix (for the phlox zone)
docs.text:
Create a distinct gritty zone in the corner/edge where phlox will live:

Component Proportion Purpose Quality loamy topsoil 40% Structure, some nutrition Coarse horticultural grit or pea gravel (3/8") 30% Rapid drainage Coarse perlite (chunky grade) 20% Drainage without compaction Coarse horticultural sand (NOT builder's sand) 10% Additional drainage

This mix drains within 30–60 seconds when watered.

docs.title: pH
docs.text:
• 6.0–7.0 (flexible — does NOT need acid)
  • Tolerates slightly acidic to slightly alkaline
  • This conflicts with azalea (4.5–5.5) — keep them in separate zones

docs.title: What It Cannot Tolerate
docs.text:
• Waterlogged soil even briefly
  • Heavy clay without amendment
  • Mulch piled against the crown (creates crown rot)

docs.title: Placement
docs.text:
• Position near drainage holes in the bed wall
  • At the outer edge of the bed (cascading over the side)
  • In the neutral-pH zone (short leg of the L), away from acid amendments

docs.title: Nutrient Needs
docs.text:
Minimal feeder — over-fertilizing causes problems.

The Spruce: "Fertilizing in the late winter or early spring will promote growth and support a more robust bloom. Feed with a general slow-release fertilizer suitable for flowering plants."

docs.title: Schedule
docs.text:
When What Late winter/early spring (Feb–Mar) One application slow-release balanced fertilizer (10-10-10 or 5-10-5) Growing season (optional) Fish emulsion at half strength every 4–6 weeks Late summer–fall No fertilizing — promotes tender growth vulnerable to damage

Warning: Excess nitrogen = lush leafy growth at expense of flowers. If plant fails to bloom, too much nitrogen or too much shade is often the culprit.

docs.title: Container/Raised Bed Feeding
docs.text: Gardening Know How: "Feed container grown phlox every other week using a general purpose, water-soluble fertilizer mixed to half strength."

docs.title: Care & Watering Cycle (Month by Month)
docs.text: Month Activity February Apply slow-release fertilizer before new growth. Trim any winter-damaged brown areas. Foliage stays green through winter in zone 7b–8a. Mar–April Peak bloom — white flowers covering the mat. No fertilizer during bloom. Water regularly if dry. May CRITICAL: Shear back by 1/3 to 1/2 immediately after bloom ends. Use sharp hedge shears uniformly across the entire mat. Mat will look bare briefly, then push vigorous new growth within 2–3 weeks. Jun–Aug Water every 3–5 days in raised bed. Monitor for spider mites during hot/dry spells. No fertilizer. Sep–Oct Good time for dividing (every 2–3 years). Light compost if desired. Reduce watering. Nov–Jan Semi-evergreen foliage persists — attractive green texture at bed edge all winter. No protection needed. No feeding.

docs.title: Post-Bloom Shearing — The Most Important Maintenance Task
docs.text:
Missouri Botanical Garden: "Cut back stems after flowering by 1/2 to maintain form and promote denser growth plus to stimulate a possible light rebloom."

Gardening Know How: "Cut the plant back by one-third to one-half after blooming to create a neater plant and encourage a second flush of blooms."

Timing: Immediately after blooms fade — typically May in zone 7b–8a.

Technique: 1. Use sharp, clean hedge shears or pruning shears 2. Shear the entire mat back by 1/3 to 1/2 — cut uniformly across the top 3. For raised bed edge plants: trim cascading runners to desired length (or let them flow freely)

Why this matters: Skipping post-bloom shearing leads to open, woody centers, reduced vigor, and an increasingly ragged mat. Regular shearing keeps it looking lush year after year.

Do NOT: - Shear in fall or late summer (removes next year's developing growth) - Shear while in bloom (wait until last flower fades)

docs.title: Growth Habit & Edge Planting
docs.text:
Ideal for raised bed edges — this is the plant's best use in your setup.

Chicago Botanic Garden: "It grows only 6 inches high, forming a dense cushion of dark green foliage 18 inches wide that will cascade over a wall."

docs.title: Growth Details
docs.text:
• Height: 4–6 inches (mat-forming)
  • Spread: 18–24 inches; 6–10 inches lateral growth per year once established
  • Cascading: Can spill 12–18 inches down the side of a raised bed
  • Semi-evergreen: Retains foliage through winter in zone 7b–8a — year-round texture

docs.title: Placement
docs.text:
• Crown 4–6 inches from the bed wall
  • Allow 18 inches of space behind for spread into the bed
  • Position on the side that gets the most sun
  • Will form a solid mat that cascades dramatically over the bed edge within 2–3 seasons

docs.title: Winter Hardiness (Zone 7b–8a)
docs.text:
Fully hardy with no protection needed.

Ball Seed rates 'Snowflake' for zones 3a–8b. Zone 7b–8a winter lows (10–20°F) will not harm established plants. The semi-evergreen foliage may look rough after hard freezes but regenerates in spring.

Heat hardiness: Actually performs better in zone 7b–8a than colder zones — native to sunny, warm-summer Appalachians. Tolerates summer heat well with adequate drainage and periodic water.

docs.title: Troubleshooting Guide
docs.text: Problem Likely Cause Solution Brown patches in mat Spider mites (webbing on undersides), root rot (mushy roots), or foliar nematodes (brown wedge patterns on leaves) Check undersides for webbing; inspect roots; nematodes = remove/destroy plant Center die-out / bare center Normal aging every 3–5 years; insufficient post-bloom shearing Divide and replant; or cut center hard and allow regrowth Failure to bloom Too much shade (<4 hrs sun); excess nitrogen fertilizer; needs division (4+ years) Move to sunnier spot; use low-N fertilizer; divide Leggy, open mat Skipped post-bloom shearing; too much shade Shear immediately post-bloom each year; ensure 6+ hrs sun Yellow foliage Overwatering or too little light Check drainage; assess sun hours Wilting in morning Root rot (if soil moist) OR underwatering (if soil dry) — opposite problems Check soil moisture and root condition Spider mites Hot, dry conditions (Jul–Aug) Strong water jets to leaf undersides; insecticidal soap; neem oil

docs.title: Spider Mites — Primary Pest
docs.text:
Missouri Botanical Garden: "Spider mites can be a problem, particularly in hot, dry conditions."

  • Signs: Fine webbing on undersides; stippled, bronze-looking leaves
  • Peak: Hot, dry July–August
  • Organic treatment: Strong water jets to undersides; insecticidal soap (avoid above 85°F); neem oil

docs.title: Foliar Nematodes
docs.text:
Missouri Botanical Garden: "Foliar nematodes can cause damage in wet/humid conditions."

  • Signs: Brown, wedge-shaped leaf patches following veins
  • Treatment: No effective chemical control. Remove and destroy affected plants. Improve air circulation. Do not replant in same soil.

docs.title: Root Rot
docs.text:
The Spruce: "Spotty leaves, soft stems, and mushy roots if planted in poorly draining and excessively moist soil."

#1 killer in raised beds when drainage is inadequate. Prevention = excellent drainage in soil mix. Once established, no recovery — remove and destroy.

docs.title: Powdery Mildew
docs.text:
Missouri Botanical Garden: "Phlox subulata and its cultivars do not seem to be bothered by powdery mildew to the same extent as most other species of phlox."

Good news — 'Snowflake' is inherently more mildew-resistant than tall garden phlox. If it appears: potassium bicarbonate, sulfur-based fungicides (not above 85°F), or neem oil.

docs.title: Raised Bed Specific Considerations
docs.text:
1. Drainage is everything. Use the gritty mix described above. Position near drainage holes.
  2. Raised beds dry faster — may need watering every 3–4 days during heat waves on concrete
  3. Edge cascade is the star feature — let it spill over the wall for dramatic spring display
  4. No mulch against the crown — unlike the azalea/winterberry zones, do NOT mulch around phlox crowns
  5. Divide every 2–3 years to prevent center die-out
  6. pH neutral zone — keep away from acid amendments intended for azalea/winterberry
